narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your full-time study plans and a solid LSAT score of 40. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 47.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance rankings compared to others in your school. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 1 was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it stands well below the typical student's mid-range position and signals potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. While your Year 1 decile of 2 also nudged the odds slightly toward failure, it had less weight. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was a standout strength, well above the average of around 37, strongly pulling the prediction toward bar exam success and showing your aptitude for legal reasoning. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, though below the typical 3.21, offered some counterbalance in favor of passing, as did your upper-middle income bracket and full-time commitment, which align with most successful applicants. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to typical admitted students because your lower decile rankings outweighed your LSAT edge, tipping the model toward concern about bar passage. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your promising test score and dedication. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles—perhaps through targeted tutoring or study habits—and consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps, and we're rooting for your next steps toward law school. Keep pushing forward.
